sentences.base_language: I wake up at seven in the morning.
sentences.target_language: Je me réveille à sept heures du matin.
sentences.grammarNote: Se réveiller is a reflexive verb: je me réveille, tu te réveilles, il se réveille. Many daily routine verbs are reflexive: se lever (to get up), se laver (to wash), s'habiller (to get dressed), se coucher (to go to bed).

sentences.base_language: I get up and go to the bathroom.
sentences.target_language: Je me lève et je vais à la salle de bain.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Je me lève** — **se lever** has a spelling change: the è (grave accent) appears when the ending is silent: je me lève, tu te lèves, il se lève. But: nous nous levons (no accent change before -ons).
**à la salle de bain** = to the bathroom — **à la** before feminine noun. Note: **la salle de bain** (no -s on bain) · **la salle de bains** (with -s) — both are accepted.

sentences.base_language: I wash my face and brush my teeth.
sentences.target_language: Je me lave le visage et je me brosse les dents.
sentences.grammarNote: With body-care verbs, French uses reflexive + definite article instead of a possessive: 'je me lave le visage' (I wash my face), not 'je lave mon visage'. The reflexive pronoun already tells us whose body it is.
'Le petit-déjeuner' = breakfast (literally 'the little lunch'). 'Le déjeuner' = lunch. 'Le dîner' = dinner. 'Le goûter' = afternoon snack (popular with children).

sentences.base_language: I drink a cup of coffee.
sentences.target_language: Je bois une tasse de café.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Je bois** = I drink — **boire** is an irregular verb: je bois, tu bois, il boit, nous buvons, vous buvez, ils boivent.
**une tasse de café** = a cup of coffee — **de** (not **du**) after a container/measure noun: une tasse de, un verre de, une bouteille de.
Contrast: **du café** (some coffee, partitive) vs **une tasse de café** (a cup of coffee, measured).

**s'habiller** = to get dressed (reflexive verb); begins with a vowel, so the reflexive pronoun elides: **je m'habille** (not *je me habille*).
**vite** = quickly (adverb) — invariable, placed after the verb. Note: **rapidement** is also correct but **vite** is much more common in speech.
**bien s'habiller** = to dress well · **s'habiller chic** = to dress up.

sentences.base_language: I say goodbye and leave the house.
sentences.target_language: Je dis au revoir et je quitte la maison.
sentences.grammarNote:
**je dis** = I say — **dire** is an irregular verb: je dis, tu dis, il dit, nous disons, vous dites, ils disent. Note the irregular vous form: **dites** (not *disez*).
**quitter** = to leave (a place): je quitte la maison · je quitte Paris. Do not use **partir de** and **quitter** interchangeably — **quitter** always takes a direct object; **partir** does not: je pars (I'm leaving, no object).

sentences.base_language: I take the metro to work every day.
sentences.target_language: Je prends le métro pour aller au travail tous les jours.
sentences.grammarNote:
**pour aller** = in order to go — **pour** + infinitive expresses purpose: je prends le métro pour aller au travail (I take the metro to get to work).
**au travail** = to/at work — **à + le = au**.
**tous les jours** = every day — compare: **chaque jour** (each day) — both are correct; **tous les jours** is more conversational.
